clinical_history: This ECG from a 35 year old woman is most consistent with which ONE of the following serum laboratory findings?
diagnosis: The ECG shows sinus rhythm at about 90/min. The QRS axis is vertical (about +85 degrees), but not frankly rightward. There are nonspecific ST-T abnormalities in the lateral leads. Most important is the (subtle!) prolongation the QT/QTc at 430msec/480 obtained using Hodges formula: QTc = QT + 1.75 (heart rate  60). The patient had renal failure with prominent hypocalcemia (6.9 mg/dL in concert with hyperphosphatemia (8.0 mg/dL) Her albumin was reduced at 2.9g/dL, but not enough to account for the hypocalcemia. Serum potassium was minimally elevated (5.1mEq/L). Hypocalcemia causes relatively uniform prolongation by stretching out the plateau (phase 2) of the ventricular action potential (corresponding to the ST-segment phase). This case is of interest because of the subtlety of the ECG findings.
